content.body: Earth is modelled as a sphere. Positions on Earth are described using latitude and longitude.

LATITUDE: Angular distance north or south of the Equator (0° to 90° N or S). Lines of latitude are parallel circles. The Equator is the largest (a great circle). Latitude lines decrease in size as you move away from equator.
LONGITUDE: Angular distance east or west of the Prime Meridian (0° to 180° E or W). Lines of longitude are half-circles (meridians) meeting at the poles. All meridians are great circles.

Arc length along a great circle = (θ/360) × 2πR
where R = 6370 km (radius of Earth), θ = angle subtended at centre

Arc length along a small circle (latitude φ) = (θ/360) × 2π × r
where r = R cos(φ) = radius of that latitude circle

content.title: Distance Along Equator
content.body:
Find distance from (0°, 20°E) to (0°, 50°E).
Δθ = 30°. On equator (great circle, r = R = 6370km)
Distance = (30/360) × 2π × 6370 = 3,335 km

questions.question: The Equator has latitude:
questions.explanation: The Equator is defined as 0° latitude — the reference line dividing Northern and Southern hemispheres.

questions.question: Lines of longitude are:
questions.explanation: Lines of longitude (meridians) are half-circles running from North Pole to South Pole.

questions.question: The Prime Meridian passes through:
questions.explanation: The Prime Meridian (0° longitude) passes through the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London.

questions.question: A great circle on Earth is:
options: A circle whose centre is Earth's centre
questions.answer: A circle whose centre is Earth's centre
questions.explanation: A great circle divides the Earth into two equal halves. Only the Equator and all meridians are great circles.

questions.question: The shortest path between two points on Earth's surface lies along a:
questions.explanation: Great circle routes are the shortest paths on a sphere's surface — used in aviation.

questions.question: Two cities at latitude 60°N. The radius of the 60°N circle relative to Earth's radius R is:
questions.explanation: Radius of latitude circle at φ = R cosφ = R cos60° = R × 0.5.

questions.question: Point A is at (30°N, 40°E), point B is at (30°N, 100°E). Angular difference in longitude =
questions.explanation: 100° − 40° = 60° difference in longitude.

questions.question: A point at (45°S, 30°W) is in which hemisphere?
questions.explanation: S = Southern hemisphere. W = Western hemisphere.

questions.question: The radius of Earth used in calculations is approximately:
questions.explanation: Earth's mean radius ≈ 6370 km (sometimes written as 6400 km).

questions.question: Distance along Equator between 10°W and 70°W (take R=6400km, π=3.14):
Lines of latitude are also called:
questions.explanation: Lines of latitude are called parallels because they are parallel to each other.

questions.question: The International Date Line is approximately at:
questions.explanation: The International Date Line runs roughly along 180° longitude — crossing it changes the calendar date.

questions.question: Kampala, Uganda is at approximately (0°N, 32°E). It lies on:
questions.explanation: Kampala is almost exactly on the Equator (actually ~0.3°N, virtually on the equator).

questions.question: Two points on same meridian, latitudes 20°N and 50°N. Angular distance =
questions.explanation: Both on same meridian → angular distance = 50° − 20° = 30°.

questions.question: Why do aircraft fly great circle routes even though they curve on a flat map?
options: Great circles are the shortest paths on a sphere
Great circles are easier to navigate
To save fuel at high altitudes only
questions.answer: Great circles are the shortest paths on a sphere
questions.explanation: On a flat (Mercator) map, great circles appear curved, but they are shorter than straight map lines.

questions.question: A ship sails from (0°, 20°E) to (0°, 80°E) along the Equator. Angular change =
questions.explanation: 80° − 20° = 60° of longitude change along the Equator.

questions.question: The Tropic of Capricorn is at latitude:
questions.explanation: Tropic of Capricorn = 23.5°S. Tropic of Cancer = 23.5°N.

questions.question: Time zones are based on:
options: Distance from prime meridian in km
questions.explanation: Every 15° of longitude = 1 hour time difference (360° ÷ 24 hours = 15°/hour).

questions.question: At the North Pole, all directions point:
questions.explanation: At the North Pole, you are at the northernmost point — every direction from there is south.

questions.question: Arc length = (θ/360) × 2πR. If θ = 45° and R = 6370km, arc length ≈
